planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring with the neck of the bulb at or just above soil surface; in zone 9 plant January–March for spring bloom or September for fall bloom. In zone 9 amaryllis bulbs can remain in the ground year-round as permanent perennials, blooming in spring (March–April) and sometimes again in fall. Established outdoor clumps in zone 9 bloom prolifically with minimal care. When grown in containers (as holiday houseplants), plant with 1/3 of the bulb above the potting mix; a single large bulb will typically produce 2 scapes each bearing 4 large flowers. Allow the foliage to grow and die back naturally after flowering to rebuild the bulb for next season.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Red Lion Amaryllis performs best with some shade protection in zone 9's summer heat, which can stress the bulb during summer dormancy; Red Lion is the defining red amaryllis cultivar — a large-flowered, velvety, deep red to scarlet selection that has become the standard commercial red amaryllis in the US market, producing the classic amaryllis flower form: massive, trumpet-shaped blooms 6–8 inches across borne in groups of 4 on thick, hollow scapes that emerge directly from the bulb. The bold, trumpet-form flowers in vivid red with a subtle lighter central stripe represent the most widely recognized and purchased amaryllis color — available in virtually every garden center and holiday gift catalog and producing a dramatic indoor or outdoor floral display that few other bulb flowers can match in sheer size and color impact. In zone 9, outdoors, Red Lion amaryllis is a permanent garden perennial rather than a temporary holiday novelty — the large bulbs naturalize in the ground, multiplying steadily over years and producing spring flower displays of increasing size as clumps develop. The combination of easy culture, spectacular flowers, and permanent naturalization in zone 9 makes amaryllis one of the most rewarding large-flowered bulbs available for Gulf South gardens.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Amaryllis as Florida Garden Bulb
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Amaryllis Naturalization in Gulf South Gardens
North Carolina State University Extension — Hippeastrum Bulbs for Southern Gardens

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring with the neck of the bulb at or just above soil surface; in zone 9 plant January–March for spring bloom. Mont Blanc naturalizes in the ground in zone 9 as a permanent perennial, multiplying steadily over years to produce increasingly impressive white flower masses in March–April. The pure white flowers show beautifully in evening and shaded garden settings where white flowers reflect and amplify available light. Allow foliage to grow and die back naturally through summer before going dormant; do not remove green leaves prematurely. As a container amaryllis, plant in well-drained potting mix with 1/3 of the bulb above the surface for traditional holiday bloom.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Mont Blanc Amaryllis performs beautifully in partial shade in zone 9 where its pure white flowers are displayed most effectively against shaded backgrounds; Mont Blanc is the definitive pure white amaryllis cultivar, producing the same massive, trumpet-shaped flowers (6–8 inches across, 4 per scape) as Red Lion amaryllis in a clean, brilliant white without markings or color variation — a pristine white that is particularly effective in evening gardens, formal white garden designs, and combination plantings where white provides a neutral, luminous contrast to colored companions. The pure white flower color works with essentially any color palette and any garden design style — mixing harmoniously with both cool-colored companions (blue, purple, silver) and warm-colored ones (red, orange, yellow) while simultaneously elevating the visual quality of each. The large flowers on sturdy scapes combine with the white color to make Mont Blanc one of the most versatile cut flower amaryllis varieties — the flowers last 2–3 weeks in a vase and the striking white color and bold scale make impressive arrangements with minimal other material. In zone 9 outdoor beds, naturalizing white amaryllis provide a reliable spring flower display that intensifies year after year as the clumps expand.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — White Amaryllis for Florida Gardens
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Amaryllis Cultivars for Gulf South
Dutch Bulb Growers Association — Hippeastrum 'Mont Blanc' Cultivar Description

planting.note: Plant divisions or corms after all frost danger has passed when soil is warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Black Coral taro grows rapidly in zone 9's warm, humid summers — reaching full size by midsummer from spring planting. The near-black foliage color is most intense in higher light conditions; deep shade produces slightly lighter green-black leaves. Provide consistent moisture, as colocasia is moisture-demanding during active growth. In zone 9 the corms often survive in the ground through mild winters with heavy mulching; dig and store in frost-free conditions to guarantee survival through colder spells.

directions: Plant in partial shade to shade in moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Black Coral taro can also be grown in full sun if moisture is consistently high, but the dramatic near-black foliage color is most effective against the lightening backdrop of shaded garden settings; Black Coral is a dark-foliaged cultivar of common taro (Colocasia esculenta) producing heart-shaped, upward-facing leaves in an extraordinary near-black color — the deepest, most saturated dark foliage available among commonly grown elephant ear varieties, with stems and leaf undersides showing deep purple-maroon tones that complement the black-green leaf surface. The near-black foliage creates a dramatic, high-contrast focal point in shade garden settings where it pairs effectively with light-colored companions — chartreuse sweet potato vine, silver-variegated ground covers, and bright-flowered shade annuals all show brilliant contrast against the dark background of Black Coral. This cultivar is particularly valuable in shade gardens precisely because the very dark foliage absorbs and amplifies the subtle light available in shaded conditions, creating a visual depth and shadow play that lighter-foliaged plants cannot achieve. In zone 9 Black Coral grows vigorously in the summer heat and humidity that is its native climate — rapidly producing increasingly large leaves through summer as the corm establishes.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Colocasia esculenta Dark-Leafed Cultivars
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Dark Elephant Ears for Gulf South Shade Gardens
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Tropical Foliage for Zone 9 Shade

planting.note: Move outdoors to a shaded location after temperatures remain above 60°F; in zone 9 place outdoors April–October and bring indoors when temperatures approach 50°F. Brasil Philodendron grows as a trailing or climbing vine, extending several feet in a single season when well-grown. Stem cuttings root easily in water or moist potting mix, making propagation simple. Outdoors on a shaded porch or pergola in zone 9, the warm summer humidity promotes rapid growth and large, vivid leaves. In zone 10–11 it can be grown as a permanent outdoor shade plant in protected locations.

directions: Plant in bright indirect light to shade — never in direct sun — in well-drained, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral, consistently moist soil; Brasil is a variegated cultivar of Heartleaf Philodendron (Philodendron hederaceum) producing the familiar heart-shaped leaves of the parent species in a striking bicolor pattern — each leaf displays a central zone of chartreuse to bright yellow-green contrasting with the dark forest green of the leaf margins, with the boundary between the two tones being irregular and painterly rather than a precise stripe. The color pattern creates a vivid, spontaneous, almost abstract effect that gives Brasil philodendron a more artful and dynamic quality than regularly striped or symmetrically patterned foliage plants. The trailing, vining habit makes Brasil excellent for hanging baskets, the edges of containers, and as a trailing accent on shaded shelves and pedestals — the long stems cascading over the container edges to create a flowing, lush effect. Heartleaf Philodendron in general is one of the most genuinely shade- and low-light-tolerant trailing plants available, surviving in conditions where most other ornamental plants decline, making Brasil one of the most practically useful shade container plants for zone 9 covered patios and indoor situations.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Philodendron hederaceum Cultivars for Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Foliage Vines for Gulf South Shade
American Horticultural Society — Philodendron Species and Cultivar Guide

planting.note: Plant after all frost danger has passed when soil and air are warm; in zone 9 plant March–May. Sun Parasol Crimson is a selection from the Sun Parasol brand mandevilla series bred for outdoor performance in warm climates. The plant twines naturally and needs a trellis, fence, pergola, or other structure to climb. In zone 9 it grows vigorously from spring through fall, blooming continuously without deadheading from first planting through first frost. In the warmest zone 9 locations the plant may survive mild winters as a semi-woody perennial; in most of zone 9 treat as an annual or move container-grown plants indoors before frost. The milky sap is irritating to some people — wear gloves when pruning.

directions: Plant in full sun to partial shade in well-drained, fertile, slightly acidic to neutral soil — Sun Parasol Crimson Mandevilla blooms most abundantly in full sun with consistent moisture; Mandevilla (Mandevilla x amabilis) is a tropical twining vine producing large, funnel-shaped flowers with five broadly overlapping petals in a classic five-lobed arrangement — the Sun Parasol Crimson selection bearing vivid crimson to deep rose-red flowers 3–4 inches across that create a striking, uninterrupted bloom display from late spring through frost. Mandevilla's combination of tropical flower size (far larger than most annual vines), glossy deep green foliage, continuous bloom without deadheading, and ability to quickly cover a trellis, pergola, or fence with an impressive tropical display makes it one of the most impactful ornamental vines for zone 9 summer gardens. The large, funnel-shaped flowers are hummingbird-attractive — the trumpet form, bright crimson color, and sweet nectar production make mandevilla a reliable hummingbird plant wherever hummingbirds occur. In zone 9's warm, humid climate, mandevilla performs as one of the finest summer flowering vines available — thriving in the heat that defeats many vine species and providing the tropical character that suits the Gulf South climate.
sources: University of Florida IFAS Extension — Mandevilla Vines for Florida
Louisiana State University AgCenter — Tropical Flowering Vines for Gulf South Landscapes
Texas A&M AgriLife Extension — Sun Parasol Mandevilla for Zone 9
